transcript.content: You are an AI tool for journalists to identify bias in their articles. Output ONLY a valid JSON array. No prose, no markdown fences. Empty array [] if no bias found. Format: [{"biasType": "...", "biasedText": "...", "explanation": "..."}]

Bias types: Spin, Unsubstantiated Claims, Opinion Statements Presented as Fact, Sensationalism/Emotionalism, Mudslinging/Ad Hominem, Mind Reading, Slant, Flawed Logic, Bias by Omission, Omission of Source Attribution, Bias by Story Choice and Placement, Subjective Qualifying Adjectives, Word Choice, Negativity Bias, Elite vs. Populist Bias.

Be cautious — present fewer, confident examples rather than more uncertain ones.

Analyze this article:

---
HEADLINE: Classes of more than 70 students amid historic declines in test scores 02:12 â¢ Source: CNN Classes o
SOURCE: CNN (Opinion)

ARTICLE:

Classes of more than 70 students amid historic declines in test scores 02:12 â¢ Source: CNN Classes of more than 70 students amid historic declines in test scores 02:12 CNN — Students who struggled through the height of the Covid-19 pandemic â between March 2020 and early 2023 â are still having trouble keeping up with course work, according to a study from the nonprofit NWEA, which focuses on education research. âThis analysis provides the most current evidence to help guide recovery efforts and resource allocations in support of schools,â an NWEA news release said. âWhile the pandemic is now deemed over, the impacts on students based on two markers, achievement in reading and mathematics, are still apparent.â Sixth-grade students in Topeka, Kansas, set up their lockers on the first day of classes in August 2022. Evert Nelson/The Capital-Journal/USA Today Network Related article Test scores for 13-year-olds drop several points since the start of pandemic, building on decade-long decline, report says The study used data from 6.7 million public school students from grades 3-8, the release said. According to the study, âIn nearly all grades, achievement gains during 2022-23 fell short of prepandemic trends, which stalled progress toward pandemic recovery.â The performance gap widened in some grades when comparing students affected by Covid-19 and those before the pandemic, the data showed. The study found that students need an additional 4.1 months to catch up in reading and 4.5 months of additional instruction to catch up in math. Among those students, Black and Hispanic students âremain furthest from recovery,â the release said. âCOVID-19 may no longer be an emergency, but we are very much still dealing with the fallout from the crisis,â said Dr. Karyn Lewis, co-author of the study and director of the Center for School and Student Progress at NWEA, according to the release. Some students in the US are struggling with math and reading after the Covid-19 pandemic, a new study shows. Jose Luis Pelaez Inc/Digital Vision/Getty Images âThese data reiterate that recovery will not be linear, easy, or quick and we cannot take our foot off the gas pedal,â Lewis said. âDisappointing as these results may be, itâs important not to lose sight of the fact that things would likely be so much worse without the enduring work of educators and schools to support students in this moment.â The NWEAâs recommendations include using local data to help students continue to recover, intervening as needed and communicating important information with families in a timely way. The NWEA describes itself as a âpioneer in educational research and assessment methodology,â with over four decades of work.
---

parsed_output.biasedText: Students who struggled through the height of the Covid-19 pandemic â between March 2020 and early 2023 â are still having trouble keeping up with course work
parsed_output.explanation: This presents as factual that all students who experienced the pandemic are 'still having trouble,' when the study shows trends and averages, not universal student struggles. This broad claim lacks nuance about variation in student performance.

parsed_output.biasedText: The headline references 'classes of more than 70 students' which appears nowhere in the article body, creating a sensational but unsupported connection to the test score data. The word 'historic' also amplifies emotional impact.
Among those students, Black and Hispanic students âremain furthest from recovery,â the release said.
parsed_output.explanation: The article mentions racial disparities but provides no context about pre-pandemic achievement gaps, socioeconomic factors, or whether these gaps widened or simply persisted, leaving readers without important contextual information.
